answer:
**Viṣṇu Smṛti chapter 67** details the daily ritual obligations of a householder, focusing on preparatory acts around the sacred fire, offerings (*bali*) to deities (e.g., Vāsudeva, Agni, directional guardians), ancestors (*piṇḍa*), outcastes (dogs, crows), and alms, with special emphasis on the worship of guests (*atithi* hospitality).[^1]

It outlines who qualifies as a true *atithi* (e.g., a transient Brāhmaṇa, not locals or those with specific business), the feeding hierarchy (family dependents first, then Brāhmaṇas, other castes with servants, friends), and the supreme spiritual merit of guest worship—surpassing Vedic study or sacrifices—while warning against neglect.[^2][^3]

[^1]: Viṣṇu Smṛti, [Chapter 67](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/67/translation/).

[^2]: Viṣṇu Smṛti, [Chapter 67](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/67/translation/#p-653828).

[^3]: Viṣṇu Smṛti, [Chapter 67](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/67/translation/#p-0f50b0).
